straight_beat.name: Harold 'Hal' Zorich
straight_beat.column:
The Reds lost again, but not without some flickers of hope. Yesterday’s 9-6 defeat to the Angels was a reminder that even in a division as competitive as the NL Central, you don’t get to take a breather. The Reds are 9-7, 1.0 game behind the Brewers, and the ledger’s still got a few pages to fill. That’s the Queen City ledger.  

The pitching staff, for all its promise, continues to flounder. Carlos Carrasco, supposed to be the anchor, lasted just five innings, surrendering four runs. He’s got the

optimist.name: Candy Vanicek
optimist.column:
The Angels had their way with us yesterday, but let’s not let that cloud the fact that Elly De La Cruz is still the reason this team keeps turning heads. You know, when the dust settled and the scoreboard read 9-6, I didn’t hear a single soul in the stands muttering about the Reds’ fate. Not one. Because Elly? He’s the heartbeat of this club, and that heartbeat’s still strong.  

Now, I’ll admit, the loss stings. The way the Angels’ bats came alive, the way our pitching stumbled like a man in a rainstorm—yeah, it’s hard to swallow. But here’s the thing: Elly didn’t just show up to the game. He showed up to *change* the game. That triple play he engineered? That’s not just a play; that’s a statement. He ran from first to third in a blink, and I swear I could hear Rose cussing from the beyond. This is Cincinnati baseball reborn.  

You see, the Reds are a machine, and machines don’t break—they recalibrate. Elly’s got that spark, that relentless drive to make every at-bat matter. He’s not just a player; he’s a reminder that this team’s got the grit to claw back. The standings? Sure, we’re a game back in the Central, but that’s just the scoreboard. The real measure is in the way Elly keeps swinging, keeps running, keeps making us believe.  

And let’s not forget the rest of the roster. Those young arms in the rotation? They’re learning, growing, and every start they

pessimist.name: Earl Huffnagle
pessimist.column:
The Reds lost again. Not in a dramatic, heartbreaking way—no, that’s saved for the playoffs, which we’re not even in yet. No, this was a game where they played like a team that’s been told, repeatedly, that they’re not good enough. The Angels scored nine runs, the Reds scored six, and the scoreboard read like a reminder of how far we’ve fallen since 1975. That year, we had a team that could win games with a .250 average and a bullpen that could throw a no-hitter in a hurricane. Now? We’re a team that can’t hold a lead, can’t develop a star, and can’t stop trading the ones we do have.  

The front office has a plan, I suppose. A plan so convoluted it’s become a comedy routine. They trade the stars we develop, then complain when the next wave of prospects doesn’t pan out. “We trade every star we develop. That’s the business model.” Yeah, that’s the model. The kids come up, they play great, they leave, and I drink and turn on the next one. It’s a cycle as old as the franchise itself, and it’s still going strong.  

Look, I get that building a team takes time. But time is something we’ve had since 1975. We’ve had decades to figure out how to keep players, how to develop talent, how to stop being the team that’s always one step behind. Instead, we’ve got a system that rewards mediocrity
